frame_label: The Makeup Paradox: Unnatural Yet Expected
generated_comment: Honestly, I have always felt women look far better when they keep it natural and presentable rather than hiding behind layers of makeup. In professional settings also, too much makeup comes across as trying too hard, but at the same time basic grooming does matter because completely neglecting your appearance does not create the right impression either. There is a difference between confidence and creating a version of yourself that is not real.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic because it imposes a double bind on women: they should be attractive and groomed, but not visibly put effort into it. It encodes the 'natural beauty' preference, excessive policing of makeup, and the deception framing that makeup creates an unreal version of a woman.
original_frame: Gendered Division of Chores via Practicality
persona_text: You are Vikram. Age 36. Married. A marketing manager in Mumbai. You are posting under your real name on LinkedIn after reading an article about companies offering household management stipends for working women. You are writing a comment about why you think this kind of policy misunderstands how households actually work and what the natural and practical division of domestic responsibility looks like in most Indian families and why this is not a gender issue but a logistics issue.
